examples: ITEL ASP pricing reflects actual product-specific cost and is more accurate than standard Xactimate line pricing
NYBI8X_OCT25 is the appropriate price list for this region and policy type
Only 2 trades involved (roofing + siding) — O&P does not apply
Siding damage limited to rear and left elevations only — front and right elevations do not show sufficient hail damage to warrant replacement
House wrap only required on elevations being replaced — no code obligation to wrap undamaged elevations
Gutters and downspouts were inspected and do not show hail damage requiring replacement
Code upgrades (ridge vent, step flashing, counterflashing) are Ordinance or Law items — payable when incurred, not upfront
Roof vent - turtle type was included in original estimate but removed in later versions; replaced with continuous ridge vent as code upgrade

Multiple items classified as code upgrades payable when incurred: continuous ridge vent, cap existing turtle vent, cut ridge slot, step flashing, and counterflashing/apron flashing - totaling $2,613.6
.019 gauge aluminum matches existing material — .024 gauge is an upgrade, not like-kind replacement

prediction.note: Based on 3 historical claims against The Hanover Insurance Group

enclosed_documents: Forensic Causation Report with annotated photography
Supplement report with line-by-line variance analysis
summary_paragraphs: Please find enclosed the complete forensic documentation package for the property at 27 Old Bethlehem Rd, Perkasie, PA 18944, USA. Our independent inspection has documented storm-related damage to the Roofing.

The enclosed reports include annotated photography of all identified damage, an Xactimate-format replacement cost estimate, and a forensic causation analysis establishing the relationship between documented weather events and observed damage.
Please do not hesitate to contact us should you require any additional information or wish to schedule a joint inspection.

code_violations.requirement: Asphalt shingles shall be installed in accordance with this section AND the manufacturer's installation instructions — adopts manufacturer spec as code-enforceable requirement
code_violations.status: Required — manufacturer spec controls fastener count, exposure, starter, drip edge, and warranty-critical details

code_violations.requirement: Drip edge required at eaves and rake edges of shingle roofs; min 2-inch overlap at joints, min 1/4-inch projection below sheathing
Underlayment shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions — ASTM D226 / D4869 / D6757 labeling required
Factory starter strip course required along eaves and rakes with pre-applied sealant strip at roof edge; field-cut shingles do not qualify as starter (no sealant = wind-uplift failure)
code_violations.status: Required — included in scope per manufacturer wind-warranty spec

code_violations.requirement: Ice barrier required in areas where the average daily temperature in January is ≤25°F or where ice damming has a history; min 2 feet inside exterior wall line (Climate Zones 5A+ including OH/NY)
Flashings shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, wherever there is a change in roof slope or direction, and around roof openings

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 47 documented findings, the property at 27 Old Bethlehem Rd, Perkasie, PA 18944, USA sustained confirmed wind storm damage from the March 16, 2026 event. The weight of evidence — including chalk-tested creased shingle tabs with sharp horizontal fold lines at the nail line, mechanically broken seal strips with clean separation, missing shingle tabs with rust-free exposed nail heads, and emergency blue tarp repairs at ridge and mid-field locations — collectively establishes wind as the singular causation mechanism. Additionally, two documented code violations (IRC R905.2.8.1 for exposed sheathing lacking weather-resistant covering at the gable peak and IRC R703.1 for displaced siding at the wall-to-roof junction) confirm that the storm event compromised the building envelope beyond the roofing system alone.

The confirmed functional wind damage to the architectural laminated composite shingle roof system and associated components requires full system replacement rather than spot repair. The structure exhibits mixed shingle types, including discontinued 3-tab profiles for which no current matching product exists, rendering partial repair infeasible under UCC and IRC material-matching requirements. Furthermore, widespread broken seal strips documented across multiple roof orientations — windward hip slopes, ridge areas, valley transitions, and rake edges — constitute systemic functional failure that cannot be addressed through targeted repairs, as resealing wind-compromised factory-applied adhesive strips does not restore original wind resistance per HAAG Engineering standards.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes (UCC/IRC R905.2.8.1 and R703.1), and industry standards, our analysis identifies the need for full replacement of the roof system, including field shingles, ridge caps, associated flashing, drip edge at the compromised gable peak, and repair of the displaced exterior wall covering at the wall-to-roof junction. The carrier's current recognized scope of $1,947.88 does not adequately address the documented conditions, as it fails to account for the full extent of functional wind damage across all affected slopes, the code-triggered repairs to exposed sheathing and displaced siding, and the material-matching constraints imposed by the presence of discontinued shingle profiles on the structure.

This forensic causation determination is grounded in HAAG Engineering wind damage assessment standards, which define creased shingle tabs at the nail line and mechanically broken seal strips as functional damage requiring replacement. The scope recommendation further aligns with NRCA guidelines for roof system evaluation, which recognize that widespread seal failure across multiple orientations constitutes systemic compromise inconsistent with spot-repair methodology. All findings documented in this report are based on physical field inspection, photographic evidence with chalk-test verification, and contractor-reported interior leak conditions corroborating exterior shingle displacement.
forensic_findings.damage_summary: Roof inspection reveals wind damage across multiple slopes of this architectural laminate shingle roof. Chalk-circled areas document creased shingle tabs, broken seal strips, and lifted leading edges consistent with wind uplift from the March 16, 2026 storm event. Damage is present on multiple roof faces including hip slopes and near ridge lines, indicating sustained high winds. The directional damage pattern and broken seal strips with clean mechanical separation confirm wind as the primary loss mechanism. Interior leak damage reported by contractor corroborates compromised shingle integrity. Full roof replacement is warranted due to widespread seal failure and functional wind damage across all documented slopes. Wind damage inspection reveals multiple chalk-marked areas identifying creased shingle tabs, broken seal strips, and missing shingles across multiple slopes. Photo 6 shows chalk-marked wind creases on 3-tab shingles at the nail line — functional damage per HAAG standards. Photo 7 shows a chalk-circled area with a broken seal strip and displaced tab. Photos 8-9 confirm directional wind damage patterns with lifted leading edges concentrated on windward slopes. Photo 10 documents a missing shingle tab with blue tarp emergency repair, confirming active leak and catastrophic wind displacement. The combination of creased tabs, broken seals, missing shingles with rust-free nail exposures, and interior leak damage confirms the March 16, 2026 wind event caused functional damage requiring full roof replacement. The property features two distinct shingle types — 3-tab on one section and architectural laminate on the main structure — both exhibiting wind damage, making partial repair infeasible due to discontinuation of matching 3-tab product and pattern incompatibility. Architectural laminate roof exhibits wind damage across multiple slopes. Ridge line shows blue tarp emergency repairs indicating active leak points. Close-up inspection reveals creased shingle tabs with broken seal strips — functional wind damage per HAAG standards. Directional damage pattern with concentrated damage at ridge and exposed upper slopes confirms wind event consistent with March 16, 2026 date of loss. Interior leak damage reported by contractor corroborates compromised waterproofing at ridge and upper field areas. Shingles show broken seal bonds and mechanical crease lines requiring full replacement — spot repair is not feasible due to widespread seal failure and color-match limitations on aged shingles. Steep-slope architectural shingle roof inspected for wind damage from March 16, 2026 storm event. Photos 16-20 document the roof from multiple angles using a Goat Steep Assist device for safe access on this high-pitch roof. Shingle field shows wind creasing with horizontal fold lines visible at nail lines, broken seal strips, and directional damage patterns consistent with wind uplift. Close-up photo 18 reveals creased tabs with granule disruption concentrated at fold lines — functional damage per HAAG standards requiring replacement. The steep pitch and widespread seal bond failures across multiple slopes confirm full roof replacement is warranted. Interior leak damage reported by contractor corroborates compromised weatherproofing from wind-displaced shingles. Roof inspection confirms wind damage to architectural laminate shingles consistent with the March 16, 2026 storm event. Multiple shingles exhibit horizontal creasing at the nail line — the #1 forensic wind indicator per HAAG standards — representing functional damage requiring replacement. Broken seal strips with clean mechanical separation confirm wind-driven tab displacement rather than age-related adhesive failure. Edge shingles at rake and hip transitions show lifted leading edges and displaced tabs. The directional damage pattern and rust-free fastener exposure confirm recent storm origin. Given the widespread seal bond failure and creased tabs across multiple slopes, spot repair is not feasible — full roof replacement is warranted. Wind damage across multiple roof slopes and building components confirms the March 16, 2026 storm event. Fascia at the gable peak is completely torn away exposing raw wood sheathing with rust-free fasteners proving recent loss. Vinyl siding at the wall-to-roof junction shows wind displacement. Shingle field exhibits creased tabs and broken seal strips marked by chalk circles. The directional concentration of damage on windward faces and exposed underlayment at rake transitions confirm a wind event requiring full roof replacement. Interior leak damage reported by contractor corroborates compromised weatherproofing from displaced components. Architectural laminate roof exhibits wind damage across multiple slopes. Chalk circles mark hail impact locations on shingle field. Ridge cap shingles show lifted/displaced edges consistent with wind uplift at the highest exposure point. Creased and broken-seal shingles documented on hip and main slopes. Multiple chalk-circled areas confirm inspector-verified storm damage from the March 16, 2026 event. The combination of ridge cap displacement, field shingle wind creasing, and hail impacts supports full roof replacement — spot repair is not feasible given damage distribution across all visible slopes. Wind damage inspection reveals multiple chalk-circled areas on architectural laminate shingles documenting broken seal strips and wind-creased tabs across multiple slopes. Interior water staining on ceiling confirms active leak resulting from wind-compromised shingles. Chalk circles mark areas where seal strips have mechanically separated and tabs have creased at the nail line — functional wind damage requiring full roof replacement. The interior leak damage corroborates the exterior findings and confirms the roof system has been breached by the March 16, 2026 wind event. Photos 41–44 document interior ceiling water stains in multiple rooms caused by wind-driven rain infiltration from the March 16, 2026 storm event. Multiple yellowish-brown water stain rings on ceilings near exterior walls and window headers confirm active roof leaks. The staining pattern—concentrated at the ceiling-to-wall junction near windows—is consistent with wind-compromised roof-to-wall flashing or wind-lifted shingles allowing water penetration at the building envelope. These interior stains corroborate the exterior wind damage and support the need for full roof replacement to resolve the leak source.

executive_summary: On March 16, 2026, the property at 27 Old Bethlehem Rd, Perkasie, PA 18944, USA sustained storm damage from a documented wind event. A forensic inspection was conducted encompassing 44 photographs across multiple roof slopes. The roofing system consists of architectural laminated composite shingles on the main structure, with a secondary section of 3-tab shingles — both systems were evaluated for wind-induced damage and functional integrity.

Inspection findings confirm widespread wind damage across multiple slopes and orientations. Chalk-circled areas document creased shingle tabs at the nail line, broken seal strips with clean mechanical separation, lifted leading edges, and missing shingle tabs with rust-free nail exposures — all consistent with acute wind uplift rather than long-term weathering. A blue tarp emergency repair at the ridge confirms active water infiltration from catastrophic shingle displacement, and interior leak damage reported at the time of loss corroborates compromised waterproofing at ridge and upper field areas. The directional damage pattern, concentrated on windward slopes and along ridge lines, is consistent with the sustained high winds of the March 16, 2026 storm event.

Full roof system replacement is warranted on both technical and code-compliance grounds. The broken seal bonds and mechanical crease lines documented across all inspected slopes constitute functional damage per HAAG Engineering standards, rendering spot repair infeasible due to the widespread distribution of compromised shingles. Furthermore, the 3-tab shingle section utilizes a product that is no longer manufactured in a matching profile and color, making code-compliant partial replacement impossible under UCC and IRC requirements for material continuity and weatherproofing performance. The aged shingle system cannot be color-matched or selectively repaired without creating differential weathering and aesthetic non-uniformity that falls below accepted industry repair standards.

The carrier's approved scope of $1,947.88 does not account for the full extent of documented wind damage, including the widespread seal strip failures across multiple slopes, the missing shingle tab with emergency tarp repair, the required replacement of the discontinuous 3-tab section, or the interior damage resulting from active leaks. Our forensic analysis, supported by 44 field photographs and 47 key damage findings, identifies a repair scope that significantly exceeds the carrier's current valuation and reflects the actual cost to restore the roof system to its pre-loss, code-compliant condition.

manufacturer_specs.requirement: 4-6 nails per shingle, ProStart starter required at eaves and rakes
manufacturer_specs.warranty_text: GAF System Plus/Silver Pledge warranty VOID without proper starter and nailing pattern

code_citation.requirement: Existing roofing materials shall be removed before applying new roofing when existing roof has two or more applications of any type of roof covering
code_citation.supplement_argument: Complete tear-off is required per code for a code-compliant installation. Overlay is not code-compliant when damage necessitates deck inspection.
code_citation.title: Reroofing — Tear-Off Requirement

manufacturer_specs.requirement: Systematic hail damage assessment criteria for functional vs cosmetic

code_citation.requirement: Roofing shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions. Manufacturer instructions carry the force of building code.
code_citation.supplement_argument: Per R905.1, manufacturer installation instructions are legally binding building code. All components specified by the manufacturer (starter, underlayment, flashing) are code-required, not optional upgrades.
code_citation.title: Manufacturer Installation Instructions = Law

line_items.description: Laminated comp shingle roofing - w/out felt
line_items.supplement_argument: Per R905.1, manufacturer installation instructions are legally binding building code. All components specified by the manufacturer (starter, underlayment, flashing) are code-required, not optional upgrades.

manufacturer_specs.requirement: Required at eaves, valleys, and around penetrations per GAF system requirements
manufacturer_specs.warranty_text: GAF warranty VOID without WeatherWatch/StormGuard at eaves and valleys

code_citation.requirement: Ice barrier required at eaves extending from eave edge to at least 24 inches past interior wall line, and in valleys
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ice & water barrier is required by code at all eaves and valleys. This is not an optional upgrade — it is a mandatory building code requirement for a code-compliant installation.
code_citation.title: Ice Barrier Requirement

code_citation.requirement: Drip edge shall be provided at eaves and gable rake edges of shingle roofs
code_citation.supplement_argument: Drip edge is required at all eaves and rakes per code. Protects fascia and deck edge from water damage. Not an optional upgrade.
code_citation.title: Drip Edge Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Drip edge required at eaves and rakes per IRC R905.2.7. Minimum 2.5 inches below sheathing, 2 inches up deck. Underlayment OVER drip edge at eaves, UNDER at rakes. 5% waste factor standard

manufacturer_specs.requirement: 4-6 nails per shingle, ProStart starter required at eaves and rakes

manufacturer_specs.warranty_text: SureStart warranty VOID without CertainTeed-approved starter

code_citation.requirement: Starter course required at eaves and rakes per manufacturer installation instructions (R905.1 — manufacturer instructions = code)
code_citation.supplement_argument: Starter course is required by ALL major manufacturers (GAF, OC, CertainTeed). Per R905.1, manufacturer instructions carry the force of code. Omitting starter voids the manufacturer warranty.
code_citation.title: Starter Course (Manufacturer Required)

line_items.supplement_argument: Starter course is required by ALL major manufacturers (GAF, OC, CertainTeed). Per R905.1, manufacturer instructions carry the force of code. Omitting starter voids the manufacturer warranty.

code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.

code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ventilation required per IRC R806 — 1 SF NFVA per 150 SF attic area (1:150 ratio). OC warranty VOID without proper ventilation. Ridge vent must be R&R during re-roofing

code_citation.requirement: Step flashing shall be used where roof planes intersect vertical walls
code_citation.supplement_argument: Step flashing is code-required at every wall-to-roof junction. When roofing is replaced, step flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight integrity per code.
code_citation.title: Step Flashing at Wall Junctions

line_items.supplement_argument: Step flashing required at all wall-to-roof intersections per IRC R905.2.8.4. Minimum 10 inches height. Siding removal mandatory for proper installation — nail horizontal flange to roof ONLY, never through siding
Counterflashing required at all roof-to-wall intersections per IRC R905.2.8

photo_integrity.stamp: PHOTO INTEGRITY VERIFIED — 100% | 20 photos analyzed, 0 flagged for manipulation indicators | DumbRoof.AI Fraud Detection Engine
photo_integrity.summary: All 20 photos pass integrity analysis. Images show a consistent, legitimate storm damage roof inspection with standard industry practices including chalk marking of hail impacts, test square documentation, and use of steep-slope safety equipment (Goat Steep Assist). Blue tarp patches on the ridge confirm active wind damage. Hail bruising and granule loss patterns appear natural and consistent across multiple roof slopes. No evidence of staged damage, digital manipulation, photo recycling, or misrepresentation detected. The inspection appears thorough and professionally conducted.
